1:
"And God blessed Noah and his sons, and said unto them, Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth." The witnesses note God blessing Noah and renewing the command to be fruitful and replenish the earth. On "God blessed Noah and his sons," the grammatical voices note the blessing. Ellicott and Gill note that "God blessed Noah and his sons" — as God blessed Adam at the first creation (1:28), so now he blesses Noah and his sons at the renewal of the world; the blessing of the new humanity; God's favor rests on the family preserved through the flood; the renewal of the creation-blessing after the judgment.

On "Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth," the reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the renewed mandate. Calvin and Henry note that "Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth" repeats the command given to Adam (1:28) — Noah and his sons are to multiply and refill the depopulated earth; the human race, reduced to eight souls, is to increase and people the world again; the mandate of fruitfulness renewed upon the new head of mankind; through Noah's family the earth would be repeopled.

On "replenish the earth," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the refilling: "replenish (fill) the earth" — the earth, emptied by the flood, is to be filled again with men; the command both blesses and obligates; God's purpose to repopulate the world through Noah; Noah as the second father of mankind (after Adam), from whom all nations would spring. The verse displays God blessing Noah and renewing the command to replenish the earth.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blessing — "God blessed Noah and his sons," God blessing the new humanity as he blessed Adam (1:28, Ellicott, Gill); "Be fruitful, and multiply, and replenish the earth," the creation-mandate renewed, the earth to be repeopled (1:28, Calvin, Henry); "replenish the earth," the emptied world refilled through Noah (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed Noah. The verse marks the blessing: on Noah and his sons. The verse displays the mandate: be fruitful. The verse displays the earth: replenished. The verse displays the humanity: renewed. The blessing displays God renewing the creation-mandate on Noah. The verse displays the Noah: blessed. The verse displays the command: renewed. The verse displays the earth: repeopled. God blessing Noah and renewing the command to replenish the earth displays the renewal of the creation-blessing: God blessed Noah and his sons and bade them be fruitful, multiply, and replenish the earth — the mandate given to Adam (1:28) renewed on the new head of mankind, the emptied world to be repeopled through Noah. The verse displays the blessed humanity, given dominion over the creatures (v.2).
2:
"And the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast of the earth, and upon every fowl of the air, upon all that moveth upon the earth, and upon all the fishes of the sea; into your hand are they delivered." The witnesses note the fear of man placed upon the creatures, delivered into his hand. On "the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast," the grammatical voices note the renewed dominion. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast of the earth... and upon all the fishes of the sea" — God places the fear and dread of man upon all the creatures; man's dominion (1:28) is renewed, but now in a new form — through fear; the creatures, which might have grown dangerous to the diminished human race, are made to dread man; the renewed lordship of man over the animals.

On "the fear... and the dread," the reformed Calvin notes the changed relation. Calvin and Gill note that the dominion is now exercised through "fear and dread" — a harsher relation than the original (the creatures now wild and apt to harm); after the fall and flood, man's rule over the animals is maintained by their instinctive fear of him; God's provision to protect the few survivors and their descendants from the beasts.

On "into your hand are they delivered," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the grant: "into your hand are they delivered" — the creatures are given into man's hand, for his use (including food, v.3); God delivers the animals to man's power and disposal; the renewed grant of dominion, now extending (v.3) to the use of animals for food; man's lordship over the creatures confirmed. The verse displays the fear of man placed upon the creatures, delivered into his hand.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the fear — "the fear of you and the dread of you shall be upon every beast... and upon all the fishes of the sea," the fear of man placed on all creatures, the renewed dominion (1:28, Keil, Gill); the dominion now through fear and dread, a changed relation after the fall (Calvin, Gill); "into your hand are they delivered," the creatures given to man's use (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the renewed dominion. The verse marks the fear: of man. The verse displays the dread: on the beasts. The verse displays the creatures: delivered. The verse displays the dominion: renewed. The grant displays man's lordship over the creatures through fear. The verse displays the beasts: fearing. The verse displays the man: lord. The verse displays the hand: given them. The fear of man placed upon the creatures, delivered into his hand, displays the renewed dominion: the fear and dread of man set upon all the creatures, and they delivered into his hand — man's lordship over the animals renewed, now through their fear, the creatures given to his use. The verse displays the delivered creatures, granted for food (v.3).
3:
"Every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you; even as the green herb have I given you all things." The witnesses note the grant of animals for food. On "Every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you," the grammatical voices note the new grant. Gill and Clarke note that "every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you" — God now grants man the animals for food; whereas at the first man was given the herbs and fruits (1:29), now (after the flood) the flesh of living creatures is added to his diet; the express grant of animal food to Noah and his descendants; the enlarged provision for man.

On "as the green herb have I given you all things," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the comparison. Calvin and Gill note that "even as the green herb have I given you all things" — as God had given the green herb (the vegetable food) at the first (1:29), so now he gives all things (the animals also) for food; the comparison marks the enlargement of the grant; man may now eat of the creatures as freely as of the herbs; God's gracious provision for the renewed humanity.

On the grant of flesh, the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the question of whether flesh was eaten before: some hold animal food was first permitted here (after the flood), others that it was eaten before but now expressly sanctioned; whichever, God here grants the use of animals for food, with the one restriction of the blood (v.4); the provision of flesh for man's sustenance, given by God. The verse displays the grant of animals for food.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the grant — "every moving thing that liveth shall be meat for you," God granting the animals for food, the enlarged provision (1:29, Gill, Clarke); "as the green herb have I given you all things," the grant enlarged from herbs to all things, including flesh (1:29, Calvin, Gill); the use of animals for food sanctioned, with the restriction of blood (v.4, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the granted food. The verse marks the grant: animals for food. The verse displays the herb: given before. The verse displays the all things: now given. The verse displays the provision: enlarged. The grant displays God giving the creatures for man's food. The verse displays the flesh: granted. The verse displays the food: enlarged. The verse displays the provision: gracious. The grant of animals for food displays God's enlarged provision for the renewed humanity: every moving thing is given for meat, even as the green herb was given before — the flesh of the creatures added to man's food, the provision enlarged from herbs to all things, with the one restriction of the blood. The verse displays the granted flesh, with the blood forbidden (v.4).
4:
"But flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat." The witnesses note the prohibition of eating blood — the crux of its meaning and force. On "flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat," the grammatical voices note the prohibition. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "flesh with the life thereof, which is the blood thereof, shall ye not eat" — though flesh is granted for food (v.3), man may not eat it with the blood (the life) in it; the blood was to be drained out before the flesh was eaten; not that the blood is itself the life, but that the life is in the blood (Leviticus 17:11); the one restriction on the grant of animal food.

On "the life thereof, which is the blood thereof," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the rationale. Calvin and Ellicott note that the prohibition rests on the blood being the seat of the life — "the life thereof, which is the blood thereof"; the blood, as the vehicle of life, was to be treated with reverence and not consumed; the prohibition teaches a reverence for life (which belongs to God) and restrains cruelty; the blood, later reserved for atonement (Leviticus 17:11), was not to be eaten.

On the force of the prohibition, interpreters divide. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and some hold the blood-prohibition is a perpetual, moral ordinance, given to all mankind in Noah, and renewed in the apostolic decree (Acts 15:20, 29, "abstain from blood and from things strangled"); binding still. Others (with the reformed) hold it was a positive/ceremonial precept, connected with the reverence for life and the use of blood in sacrifice, which under the gospel is no longer binding as a ceremonial law (the decree of Acts 15 being a temporary accommodation). The verse displays the prohibition of eating blood.

The witnesses agree the substance: though flesh is granted, the blood (the life) is not to be eaten, out of reverence for the life that is in it (Keil, Gill, Calvin, Ellicott). The force bears a crux: a perpetual, moral ordinance given to all mankind and renewed in Acts 15:20, 29, binding still (Clarke) vs a positive/ceremonial precept, abrogated under the gospel (the reformed reading). The verse displays the blood-prohibition crux. The verse marks the flesh: granted. The verse displays the blood: forbidden. The verse displays the life: in the blood. The verse displays the debate: moral or ceremonial. The prohibition displays the blood reserved and not eaten. The verse displays the blood: not eaten. The verse displays the reverence: for life. The verse displays the ordinance: given. The prohibition of eating blood displays reverence for the life that is in it, and a crux: flesh with its life, the blood, was not to be eaten — read as a perpetual moral ordinance binding still (Acts 15:29; Clarke) or as a ceremonial precept abrogated under the gospel; the blood, the seat of life, reserved and not consumed. The verse displays the forbidden blood, with the requiring of man's blood (vv.5-6).
5:
"And surely your blood of your lives will I require; at the hand of every beast will I require it, and at the hand of man; at the hand of every man's brother will I require the life of man." The witnesses note God requiring an account for the blood of man. On "your blood of your lives will I require," the grammatical voices note the sanctity of human blood. Ellicott and Gill note that "surely your blood of your lives will I require" — God will require (call to account) for the shedding of human blood; the life of man is precious to God, and he will avenge its unlawful taking; the sacredness of human life, which may not be shed without God requiring it.

On "at the hand of every beast will I require it," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the beast. JFB and Gill note that "at the hand of every beast will I require it" — even a beast that kills a man would be required to answer (the beast that slew a man was to be put to death, Exodus 21:28); the life of man is so sacred that even an animal shedding it is held accountable; God's care for human life.

On "at the hand of man; at the hand of every man's brother will I require the life of man," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the murderer: "at the hand of man... every man's brother will I require the life of man" — God will require the life of man at the hand of the murderer; the man who sheds his brother's blood must answer with his own; every man is his brother's keeper, and the murderer is held accountable; the ground for the institution of capital justice (v.6). The verse displays God requiring an account for the blood of man.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the requiring — "your blood of your lives will I require," God requiring an account for human blood, the sacredness of life (Ellicott, Gill); "at the hand of every beast will I require it," even the beast that kills a man held accountable (Exodus 21:28, JFB, Gill); "at the hand of every man's brother will I require the life of man," the murderer held accountable (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the required blood. The verse marks the blood: required. The verse displays the beast: accountable. The verse displays the man: the murderer. The verse displays the sanctity: of life. The requiring displays God avenging the unlawful shedding of human blood. The verse displays the blood: avenged. The verse displays the life: sacred. The verse displays the account: required. God requiring an account for the blood of man displays the sanctity of human life: God will require the blood of man, at the hand of beast and of man, of every man's brother — the life of man so sacred that its unlawful shedding is avenged, the ground for the law of capital justice. The verse displays the required blood, by man to be requited (v.6).
6:
"Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed: for in the image of God made he man." The witnesses note the law of capital justice, grounded in the image of God — the crux of "by man." On "Whoso sheddeth man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed," interpreters expound the precept. The reformed Gill and the dominant reading hold this is the institution of capital justice through human government: "by man shall his blood be shed" — the murderer is to be put to death "by man," that is, by the hand of the civil magistrate (the Targum: "by the sentence of the judges"); God here establishes human government and the magistrate's authority to punish murder with death; the death penalty for murder is divinely instituted, committed to man (the magistrate) to execute; the foundation of civil order and the sword of justice (Romans 13:4). A second reading takes "by man shall his blood be shed" as a prediction or general principle (the murderer will himself fall by violence) rather than a command; but the dominant view reads it as the divine institution of judicial capital punishment.

On "for in the image of God made he man," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and reformed Calvin note the ground. Clarke and Calvin note that the reason given — "for in the image of God made he man" — grounds the sanctity of human life in the divine image; man, even fallen, still bears the image of God (a real, abiding dignity); to shed man's blood is to assault God's image; the murder of a man is so heinous, and its punishment so just, because man is made in God's image; the abiding image of God in man is the basis of the sanctity of life and the severity of the penalty.

On the precept, the grammatical-historical note the foundation of justice: the verse is the charter of human government and capital justice, given to all mankind in Noah; the magistrate bears the sword by divine appointment; the punishment of murder with death rests on the dignity of man as God's image-bearer. The verse displays the law of capital justice, grounded in the image of God.

The witnesses agree the substance: the shedder of man's blood is to be put to death, because man is made in God's image (Gill, Clarke, Calvin, Ellicott). The phrase "by man shall his blood be shed" bears a crux: the divine institution of capital justice through human government — the magistrate authorized to punish murder with death (the dominant reading — Gill; the Targum; Romans 13:4) vs a prediction/general principle that the murderer will himself fall by violence. The verse displays the capital-justice crux. The verse marks the murderer: to be put to death. The verse displays the debate: institution or prediction. The verse displays the image: of God in man. The verse displays the ground: man's dignity. The precept displays the sanctity of life upheld by capital justice. The verse displays the blood: requited. The verse displays the magistrate: the sword. The verse displays the image: assaulted by murder. The law of capital justice, grounded in the image of God, displays the charter of human government and a crux: whoso sheds man's blood, by man shall his blood be shed, for man is made in God's image — read as the divine institution of judicial capital punishment through the magistrate (Gill; Romans 13:4) or as a prediction of the murderer's fall; the sanctity of human life resting on the abiding image of God in man. The verse displays the instituted justice, with the renewed mandate (v.7).
7:
"And you, be ye fruitful, and multiply; bring forth abundantly in the earth, and multiply therein." The brief verse repeats the command to be fruitful and multiply. The grammatical voices note the renewed mandate. Gill and Calvin note that "be ye fruitful, and multiply; bring forth abundantly in the earth, and multiply therein" — God repeats the command of v.1 (and of 1:28) — Noah and his sons are to be fruitful, multiply, and fill the earth abundantly; the renewed mandate of fruitfulness; after the digression on blood and justice (vv.4-6), God returns to the blessing of multiplication; the earth is to be repeopled.

On "be ye fruitful, and multiply," the reformed Calvin notes the repeated blessing. Calvin and Gill note that the command, repeated, presses the duty and the blessing of increase; the depopulated earth is to be filled; the human race, through Noah's family, is to multiply abundantly; God's purpose for the repopulation of the world.

On "bring forth abundantly in the earth, and multiply therein," the grammatical-historical note the abundance: "bring forth abundantly... and multiply therein" — the increase is to be abundant; the earth is to teem again with men; the renewed blessing of fruitfulness urges the multiplying of the race; the repopulation of the world through Noah's descendants, that the earth might be filled. The verse displays the repeated command to be fruitful and multiply.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the mandate — "be ye fruitful, and multiply; bring forth abundantly in the earth, and multiply therein," the command of v.1 (and 1:28) repeated (Gill, Calvin); the repeated blessing of increase pressing the duty (Calvin, Gill); "bring forth abundantly... and multiply therein," the abundant increase repeopling the earth (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the renewed mandate. The verse marks the command: be fruitful. The verse displays the multiply: abundantly. The verse displays the earth: filled. The verse displays the race: increasing. The mandate displays God pressing the multiplication of the race. The verse displays the fruitful: command. The verse displays the increase: abundant. The verse displays the earth: repeopled. The repeated command to be fruitful and multiply displays the renewed blessing of increase: be fruitful, multiply, and bring forth abundantly in the earth — the mandate of v.1 (and 1:28) repeated, the depopulated earth to be filled abundantly through Noah's descendants. The verse displays the renewed mandate, before the covenant (v.8).
8:
"And God spake unto Noah, and to his sons with him, saying," The very brief verse introduces God's covenant-word to Noah and his sons. The grammatical voices note the address. Gill and Henry note that "God spake unto Noah, and to his sons with him, saying" — God addressed Noah and his sons together, to establish his covenant with them (vv.9-17); the introduction to the covenant-promise; God speaks to the new heads of mankind, to assure them of his gracious engagement; the covenant is made with Noah and his sons and their seed.

On "unto Noah, and to his sons with him," the puritan Henry notes the household. Henry notes that God spoke to Noah "and to his sons with him" — the covenant embraces not Noah only but his sons (and their seed, v.9); the whole family of the new humanity is included; God's covenant of preservation extends to all mankind springing from Noah.

On "saying," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the covenant to follow: "saying" — God's covenant-word follows (vv.9-11); God graciously condescends to make a covenant with Noah, his sons, and all living creatures; the assurance of God's promise that no flood would again destroy the earth; the covenant of God's faithfulness to the renewed creation. The verse introduces God's covenant-word to Noah and his sons.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the address — "God spake unto Noah, and to his sons with him, saying," God addressing Noah and his sons to establish the covenant (vv.9-17, Gill, Henry); "unto Noah, and to his sons with him," the covenant embracing the whole household and their seed (Henry); "saying," the covenant-word to follow (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the speaking God. The verse marks the speaking: to Noah and his sons. The verse displays the covenant: to be made. The verse displays the household: included. The verse displays the promise: coming. The word displays God addressing the new heads of mankind. The verse displays the God: speaking. The verse displays the sons: addressed. The verse displays the covenant: introduced. God's covenant-word to Noah and his sons displays the gracious address: God spoke to Noah and his sons, to establish his covenant with them — the introduction to the covenant of preservation, God graciously engaging himself to the new humanity. The verse displays the addressed household, with the covenant established (v.9).
9:
"And I, behold, I establish my covenant with you, and with your seed after you;" The witnesses note God establishing his covenant with Noah and his seed. On "I, behold, I establish my covenant with you," the grammatical voices note the divine engagement. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "I, behold, I establish my covenant with you" — God himself, emphatically ("I, behold, I"), establishes (confirms, sets up) his covenant with Noah; the covenant is God's own gracious act and engagement; the doubled "I" marks the certainty and the divine initiative; God binds himself by covenant.

On "my covenant," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the covenant. Ellicott and Gill note that "my covenant" — the covenant God now establishes (the covenant of preservation, that no flood would again destroy the earth, v.11); God's gracious, unconditional promise; the covenant rests on God's faithfulness, not on man's merit.

On "with you, and with your seed after you," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the parties: "with you, and with your seed after you" — the covenant is made with Noah and with his descendants (all mankind after him); it embraces the whole human race springing from Noah, and (v.10) the living creatures; the covenant of preservation extends to all generations; God's faithfulness pledged to mankind and the creation for all time. The verse displays God establishing his covenant with Noah and his seed.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the engagement — "I, behold, I establish my covenant with you," God emphatically establishing his covenant, the divine initiative (Keil, Gill); "my covenant," the covenant of preservation resting on God's faithfulness (Ellicott, Gill); "with you, and with your seed after you," the covenant embracing all mankind after Noah (Wesley). The verse displays the established covenant. The verse marks the covenant: established. The verse displays the I: emphatic. The verse displays the seed: after Noah. The verse displays the faithfulness: God's. The covenant displays God engaging himself to Noah and his seed. The verse displays the God: establishing. The verse displays the covenant: his. The verse displays the seed: included. God establishing his covenant with Noah and his seed displays the divine engagement: I, even I, establish my covenant with you and your seed after you — God's own gracious act, binding himself by covenant to Noah and all his descendants, the covenant of preservation resting on God's faithfulness. The verse displays the established covenant, extended to all living creatures (v.10).
10:
"And with every living creature that is with you, of the fowl, of the cattle, and of every beast of the earth with you; from all that go out of the ark, to every beast of the earth." The brief verse notes the covenant extended to every living creature. The grammatical voices note the breadth. Gill and Calvin note that "with every living creature that is with you, of the fowl, of the cattle, and of every beast of the earth... from all that go out of the ark, to every beast of the earth" — the covenant embraces not only Noah and his seed but every living creature: the fowls, cattle, and beasts that came out of the ark, and all the beasts of the earth; the covenant of preservation extends to the whole animal creation; God's faithfulness pledged to all living things.

On "every living creature... of the fowl, of the cattle, and of every beast," the witnesses note the creatures. Gill notes that the covenant includes all the kinds of creatures preserved in the ark — fowls, cattle, beasts; the animal creation, saved through the flood, is comprehended in the covenant; God's care extends to the creatures.

On "from all that go out of the ark, to every beast of the earth," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the universal scope: "from all that go out of the ark, to every beast of the earth" — the covenant reaches all living creatures, those preserved in the ark and their descendants throughout the earth; the comprehensive scope of the covenant of preservation; God pledges that no flood would destroy the earth and its creatures again; the whole creation is held in God's covenant faithfulness. The verse displays the covenant extended to every living creature.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the breadth — "with every living creature that is with you... from all that go out of the ark, to every beast of the earth," the covenant embracing all living creatures (Gill, Calvin); "of the fowl, of the cattle, and of every beast," the kinds preserved in the ark included (Gill); "from all that go out of the ark, to every beast of the earth," the universal scope of the covenant (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the covenant's breadth. The verse marks the creatures: every living. The verse displays the kinds: fowl, cattle, beast. The verse displays the ark: all that go out. The verse displays the scope: universal. The covenant displays God's pledge embracing the whole creation. The verse displays the creatures: included. The verse displays the covenant: universal. The verse displays the faithfulness: to all. The covenant extended to every living creature displays the breadth of God's pledge: the covenant made with every living creature, the fowl, cattle, and beasts from the ark and all the earth — the covenant of preservation embracing the whole animal creation, God's faithfulness pledged to all living things. The verse displays the comprehended creation, with the promise of no more flood (v.11).
11:
"And I will establish my covenant with you; neither shall all flesh be cut off any more by the waters of a flood; neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth." The brief verse notes the covenant promise that no flood shall again destroy the earth. The grammatical voices note the substance of the covenant. Gill and JFB note that "I will establish my covenant with you; neither shall all flesh be cut off any more by the waters of a flood; neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth" — the content of the covenant is God's promise that all flesh shall not again be cut off by a flood, nor shall a flood again destroy the earth; God pledges never again to send such a universal deluge; the assurance of the world's preservation from another flood.

On "neither shall all flesh be cut off any more by the waters of a flood," the reformed and grammatical-historical note the promise. JFB and Gill note that all flesh shall never again be cut off by a flood; the universal destruction by water would not be repeated (8:21); God's faithful promise secures the creation against another deluge; the world rests on God's pledge.

On "neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and witnesses note the security: "neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth" — no flood would again destroy the earth; the promise is sure and everlasting (v.16); though the world might fear another deluge, God's covenant forbids it; the security of the earth from a flood-judgment rests on God's faithful word; (the final judgment would be by fire, 2 Peter 3:7, not by water). The verse displays the covenant promise that no flood shall again destroy the earth.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the promise — "neither shall all flesh be cut off any more by the waters of a flood; neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth," God's pledge that no flood would again destroy the earth (8:21, Gill, JFB); the universal destruction by water not repeated (JFB, Gill); "neither shall there any more be a flood to destroy the earth," the security of the earth on God's word (Wesley). The verse displays the covenant promise. The verse marks the covenant: established. The verse displays the flesh: not cut off. The verse displays the flood: no more. The verse displays the earth: secured. The promise displays the world preserved from another flood. The verse displays the flood: forbidden. The verse displays the earth: safe. The verse displays the pledge: sure. The covenant promise that no flood shall again destroy the earth displays the security of the world: all flesh shall not be cut off again by a flood, nor a flood again destroy the earth — God's faithful pledge securing the creation against another deluge, the world resting on his covenant word. The verse displays the secured earth, with the rainbow as its token (vv.12-13).
12:
"And God said, This is the token of the covenant which I make between me and you and every living creature that is with you, for perpetual generations:" The witnesses note God appointing a token of the covenant for all generations. On "This is the token of the covenant," the grammatical voices note the sign. Ellicott and Gill note that "This is the token (sign) of the covenant which I make" — God appoints a visible token or sign of his covenant (the rainbow, v.13); the sign would confirm and seal the promise; God graciously condescends to give a visible pledge of his word; the token is the seal of the covenant.

On "between me and you and every living creature that is with you," the reformed Calvin and puritan Henry note the parties. Calvin and Henry note that the covenant is "between me and you and every living creature" — God, Noah and his seed, and all living creatures; the token seals the covenant made with the whole creation; the sign is for the assurance of all; God's faithfulness visibly pledged to mankind and the creatures.

On "for perpetual generations," the grammatical-historical note the perpetuity: "for perpetual generations" — the token and the covenant are for all generations, throughout the duration of the world; the sign would be a standing, perpetual pledge; every generation would see the token and be assured of God's promise; the everlasting covenant (v.16) sealed by a perpetual sign. The verse displays God appointing a token of the covenant for all generations.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the token — "This is the token of the covenant which I make," God appointing a visible sign to seal the covenant (the rainbow, v.13, Ellicott, Gill); "between me and you and every living creature," the covenant with the whole creation sealed (Calvin, Henry); "for perpetual generations," the token a standing, perpetual pledge (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the appointed token. The verse marks the token: of the covenant. The verse displays the parties: God, Noah, the creatures. The verse displays the perpetuity: all generations. The verse displays the seal: visible. The appointing displays God sealing the covenant with a perpetual sign. The verse displays the token: given. The verse displays the covenant: sealed. The verse displays the generations: all. God appointing a token of the covenant for all generations displays the visible sealing of his promise: God set a token of the covenant between himself, Noah, and every living creature, for perpetual generations — the visible sign that would confirm and seal his word to all generations. The verse displays the appointed token, named as the bow (v.13).
13:
"I do set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be for a token of a covenant between me and the earth." The witnesses note the rainbow appointed as the token of the covenant — the crux of the bow. On "I do set my bow in the cloud," interpreters note the bow. The reformed Gill and the grammatical-historical hold that the rainbow, a natural phenomenon, is now appointed as the covenant-sign: "I do set (I have set) my bow in the cloud" — the bow (the rainbow), formed by the natural causes (the sun shining on the cloud and rain), is now constituted by God as the token of his covenant; God calls it "my bow," not because newly created, but because he appoints it to be a sign; the dominant view holds the rainbow existed in nature before but is here invested with new, covenantal significance. A second view holds that the rainbow was now first created — that there had been no rain (or no rainbow) before the flood, and God now made the bow to appear as a new sign; but most (noting the natural causes) take it as the existing phenomenon now consecrated as a sign.

On "my bow in the cloud," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the fitness. Clarke and Gill note that the bow is fitly chosen — appearing in the cloud after rain, when men might fear a flood, it speaks of God's promise; the bow, seen against the very cloud that brings rain, assures that the rain will not become a flood; the natural beauty of the rainbow made the bearer of a gracious promise.

On "a token of a covenant between me and the earth," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the sign: "a token of a covenant between me and the earth" — the bow is the sign of God's covenant with the earth; whenever it appears, it testifies to God's promise that no flood would destroy the earth; the visible pledge of God's faithfulness; the rainbow, the messenger of God's covenant, assuring the world of his mercy. The verse displays the rainbow appointed as the token of the covenant.

The witnesses agree the substance: God appoints the rainbow in the cloud as the token of his covenant with the earth, assuring no flood (Gill, Clarke, Calvin). The bow bears a crux: the rainbow existed in nature (formed by sun and cloud) and is here invested with new covenantal significance (the dominant view — Gill) vs the rainbow was now first created as a new sign (the view that there was no rainbow before the flood). The verse displays the rainbow crux. The verse marks the bow: set in the cloud. The verse displays the debate: existing sign or new creation. The verse displays the token: of the covenant. The verse displays the earth: assured. The appointing displays God making the bow the sign of his promise. The verse displays the bow: appointed. The verse displays the cloud: its place. The verse displays the covenant: sealed. The rainbow appointed as the token of the covenant displays the visible pledge and a crux: God set his bow in the cloud as the token of his covenant with the earth — read by the dominant view as the natural rainbow now invested with covenantal significance (Gill), and by another as the bow first created as a new sign; the bow, seen against the rain-cloud, assuring the world that no flood would destroy it. The verse displays the appointed bow, remembered by God (vv.14-15).
14:
"And it shall come to pass, when I bring a cloud over the earth, that the bow shall be seen in the cloud:" The very brief verse notes the bow appearing in the cloud. The grammatical voices note the occasion. Gill notes that "when I bring a cloud over the earth, that the bow shall be seen in the cloud" — whenever God brings a cloud (the rain-cloud) over the earth, the bow would appear in it; the rainbow is seen against the cloud, when the sun shines on the falling rain; the very cloud that might bring fear of a flood bears the bow of promise; the occasion of the sign's appearing.

On "when I bring a cloud over the earth," the witnesses note God's hand. Gill notes that God himself "brings a cloud over the earth" — the clouds and rain are under God's command; when he sends the rain-cloud, he also sets the bow in it; God governs both the cloud (which might fear) and the bow (which assures); the providence of God in the natural order.

On "the bow shall be seen in the cloud," the grammatical-historical note the assurance: "the bow shall be seen in the cloud" — the appearing of the bow in the rain-cloud is the standing testimony of God's promise; whenever men see the bow against the cloud, they are assured that the rain will not become a flood; the sign, regularly appearing, perpetually confirms the covenant; the bow seen is the pledge remembered (v.15). The verse displays the bow appearing in the cloud.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the occasion — "when I bring a cloud over the earth, that the bow shall be seen in the cloud," the bow appearing whenever God brings the rain-cloud (Gill); "when I bring a cloud over the earth," God commanding the cloud and setting the bow in it (Gill); "the bow shall be seen in the cloud," the appearing bow the standing testimony of God's promise (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the appearing bow. The verse marks the cloud: brought by God. The verse displays the bow: seen. The verse displays the occasion: the rain-cloud. The verse displays the assurance: of the promise. The appearing displays the bow against the cloud assuring God's pledge. The verse displays the cloud: brought. The verse displays the bow: appearing. The verse displays the promise: confirmed. The bow appearing in the cloud displays the standing token of the covenant: when God brings a cloud over the earth, the bow is seen in it — the very rain-cloud bearing the bow of promise, the sign regularly appearing to assure the world that no flood would come. The verse displays the appearing bow, prompting God's remembrance (v.15).
15:
"And I will remember my covenant, which is between me and you and every living creature of all flesh; and the waters shall no more become a flood to destroy all flesh." The brief verse notes God remembering his covenant when he sees the bow. The grammatical voices note the divine remembrance. Gill and Calvin note that "I will remember my covenant, which is between me and you and every living creature of all flesh" — when the bow appears, God (speaking after the manner of men) "remembers" his covenant; not that God forgets (he never forgets), but that the bow is the appointed token at which God, as it were, calls his promise to mind and acts on it; the sign assures both God's faithful regard and man's confidence.

On "I will remember my covenant," the reformed Calvin notes the anthropomorphism. Calvin and Gill note that God's "remembering" is spoken in human terms — the bow is a reminder, not for God (who cannot forget) but as the seal of his sure promise; the language accommodates the divine to human understanding; God's faithfulness is certain, and the bow seals it.

On "the waters shall no more become a flood to destroy all flesh," the grammatical-historical note the promise: "the waters shall no more become a flood to destroy all flesh" — the substance of the remembered covenant is that the waters would never again become a flood to destroy all flesh; whenever the bow appears, the promise is sealed afresh; the world is assured that the rain will not become a deluge; God's faithful pledge secures all flesh from another flood. The verse displays God remembering his covenant when he sees the bow.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the remembrance — "I will remember my covenant... between me and you and every living creature," God remembering (an anthropomorphism, not that he forgets) at the appearing of the bow (Gill, Calvin); "I will remember my covenant," the bow the seal of God's sure promise (Calvin, Gill); "the waters shall no more become a flood to destroy all flesh," the promise that no flood would destroy all flesh (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the remembered covenant. The verse marks the remembrance: of the covenant. The verse displays the bow: the token. The verse displays the waters: no more a flood. The verse displays the promise: sealed. The remembrance displays God's faithfulness sealed by the bow. The verse displays the covenant: remembered. The verse displays the flood: forbidden. The verse displays the flesh: secured. God remembering his covenant when he sees the bow displays the sealed faithfulness of God: God remembers his covenant at the appearing of the bow, and the waters shall no more become a flood to destroy all flesh — the bow the seal of God's sure promise (an anthropomorphism, not that God forgets), assuring the world against another deluge. The verse displays the sealed covenant, an everlasting pledge (v.16).
16:
"And the bow shall be in the cloud; and I will look upon it, that I may remember the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh that is upon the earth." The brief verse notes the bow as the seal of the everlasting covenant. The grammatical voices note the standing sign. Gill notes that "the bow shall be in the cloud; and I will look upon it, that I may remember the everlasting covenant" — the bow stands in the cloud as the perpetual sign; God "looks upon it" (an anthropomorphism) to "remember" his everlasting covenant; the bow is the standing seal of the covenant between God and all living creatures; the perpetual, visible pledge of God's faithfulness.

On "I will look upon it, that I may remember," the witnesses note the anthropomorphism. Gill notes that God's "looking upon" the bow and "remembering" the covenant are spoken in human terms — not that God needs a reminder, but that the bow is the appointed seal at which his promise is, as it were, called to mind; the language assures man of God's faithful regard to his covenant.

On "the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature of all flesh," the grammatical-historical note the perpetuity: "the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature" — the covenant is everlasting (enduring as long as the earth, 8:22); it embraces God and all living creatures; the bow seals this perpetual covenant; God's faithfulness to the whole creation is pledged forever; the standing bow testifies to the everlasting covenant. The verse displays the bow as the seal of the everlasting covenant.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the seal — "the bow shall be in the cloud; and I will look upon it, that I may remember the everlasting covenant," the bow the perpetual seal of the covenant (Gill); "I will look upon it, that I may remember," God's looking and remembering an anthropomorphism, assuring his faithful regard (Gill); "the everlasting covenant between God and every living creature," the perpetual covenant with all creation sealed by the bow (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the everlasting covenant. The verse marks the bow: in the cloud. The verse displays the looking: God's. The verse displays the covenant: everlasting. The verse displays the creatures: all flesh. The bow displays the perpetual seal of God's covenant with the creation. The verse displays the bow: standing. The verse displays the covenant: everlasting. The verse displays the seal: perpetual. The bow as the seal of the everlasting covenant displays the perpetual pledge of God's faithfulness: the bow in the cloud, on which God looks to remember the everlasting covenant between him and every living creature — the standing, visible seal of God's perpetual covenant with the whole creation. The verse displays the everlasting covenant, confirmed to Noah (v.17).
17:
"And God said unto Noah, This is the token of the covenant, which I have established between me and all flesh that is upon the earth." The brief verse notes God confirming the token of the covenant to Noah. The grammatical voices note the confirmation. Gill and JFB note that "This is the token of the covenant, which I have established between me and all flesh that is upon the earth" — God repeats and confirms to Noah that the bow is the token of the covenant he has established; the repetition seals the assurance; God confirms his gracious promise to Noah and all flesh; the covenant is fixed and certain.

On "This is the token of the covenant," the witnesses note the seal. Gill notes that God again names the bow "the token of the covenant" — the confirmation of the sign; the bow is the appointed, certain pledge of God's word; the repeated declaration seals the covenant to Noah's assurance.

On "which I have established between me and all flesh that is upon the earth," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the scope: "between me and all flesh that is upon the earth" — the covenant is established between God and all flesh (all living creatures); the universal scope of the covenant of preservation; God's faithfulness pledged to the whole creation; the bow seals God's promise to all flesh upon the earth. The verse displays God confirming the token of the covenant to Noah.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the confirmation — "This is the token of the covenant, which I have established between me and all flesh," God confirming the bow as the token of the covenant (Gill, JFB); "This is the token of the covenant," the repeated declaration sealing the assurance (Gill); "between me and all flesh that is upon the earth," the universal scope of the covenant (Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the confirmed token. The verse marks the token: confirmed. The verse displays the covenant: established. The verse displays the flesh: all. The verse displays the assurance: sealed. The confirmation displays God sealing the covenant to Noah and all flesh. The verse displays the token: named. The verse displays the covenant: fixed. The verse displays the flesh: assured. God confirming the token of the covenant to Noah displays the sealed assurance of God's promise: God told Noah, This is the token of the covenant I have established between me and all flesh — the repeated confirmation sealing God's gracious promise to Noah and the whole creation. The verse closes the covenant, before the sons of Noah and the vineyard (vv.18-20).
18:
"And the sons of Noah, that went forth of the ark, were Shem, and Ham, and Japheth: and Ham is the father of Canaan." The witnesses note the three sons of Noah, and that Ham was the father of Canaan. On "the sons of Noah, that went forth of the ark, were Shem, and Ham, and Japheth," the grammatical voices note the three sons. Ellicott and Gill note that "the sons of Noah, that went forth of the ark, were Shem, and Ham, and Japheth" — the three sons who came out of the ark are named again (as the heads of the post-flood race); from these three the whole earth would be peopled (v.19); the genealogy turns to the three sons and their descendants (ch. 10), and to the incident of Noah's drunkenness and Ham's sin (vv.20-27).

On "Shem, and Ham, and Japheth," the puritan Henry notes the heads of the race. Henry and Gill note that Shem, Ham, and Japheth are the three from whom all nations would descend; the renewed humanity springs from these three; the line of promise continues through Shem (v.26).

On "and Ham is the father of Canaan," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the note about Canaan: "and Ham is the father of Canaan" — the text notes that Ham was the father of Canaan; this is added in anticipation of the curse on Canaan (v.25); the mention of Canaan prepares for the prophecy concerning him; the connection of Ham's sin (v.22) with the curse on his son Canaan (v.25) is here foreshadowed. The verse displays the three sons of Noah, and that Ham was the father of Canaan.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sons — "the sons of Noah, that went forth of the ark, were Shem, and Ham, and Japheth," the three sons, the heads of the post-flood race (Ellicott, Gill); "Shem, and Ham, and Japheth," the three from whom all nations descend, the line through Shem (Henry, Gill); "and Ham is the father of Canaan," the note anticipating the curse on Canaan (v.25, Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the three sons. The verse marks the sons: Shem, Ham, Japheth. The verse displays the ark: gone forth. The verse displays the Ham: father of Canaan. The verse displays the race: from these three. The genealogy displays the three sons and the note of Canaan. The verse displays the sons: named. The verse displays the Canaan: noted. The verse displays the race: descending. The three sons of Noah, and that Ham was the father of Canaan, display the heads of the renewed humanity and the note anticipating the curse: the sons of Noah were Shem, Ham, and Japheth, and Ham was the father of Canaan — the three from whom the whole earth was peopled, with the note of Canaan foreshadowing the prophecy (v.25). The verse displays the three sons, who overspread the earth (v.19).
19:
"These are the three sons of Noah: and of them was the whole earth overspread." The very brief verse notes the whole earth peopled from Noah's three sons. The grammatical voices note the repopulation. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "These are the three sons of Noah: and of them was the whole earth overspread" — from the three sons of Noah (Shem, Ham, Japheth), the whole earth was overspread (peopled); all the nations of the world descend from these three; the human race, reduced to eight souls, was renewed and multiplied from Noah's sons; the unity of mankind in their descent from Noah.

On "these are the three sons of Noah," the witnesses note the heads. Gill notes that Shem, Ham, and Japheth are the three heads of the post-flood race; the genealogy of the nations (ch. 10) traces all peoples to these three; the whole human family springs from them.

On "of them was the whole earth overspread," the grammatical-historical note the unity of mankind: "of them was the whole earth overspread" — the whole earth was peopled from Noah's three sons; all the nations and races of mankind descend from this one family; the unity of the human race (Acts 17:26, "of one blood all nations"); the repopulation of the world from the three sons of Noah. The verse displays the whole earth peopled from Noah's three sons.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the repopulation — "These are the three sons of Noah: and of them was the whole earth overspread," the whole earth peopled from the three sons (Keil, Gill); "these are the three sons of Noah," the three heads of the post-flood race (Gill); "of them was the whole earth overspread," the unity of mankind in their descent from Noah (Acts 17:26, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the peopled earth. The verse marks the sons: three. The verse displays the earth: overspread. The verse displays the nations: from these three. The verse displays the unity: of mankind. The repopulation displays the whole earth peopled from Noah's sons. The verse displays the sons: heads. The verse displays the earth: peopled. The verse displays the race: one. The whole earth peopled from Noah's three sons displays the unity and renewal of mankind: from the three sons of Noah the whole earth was overspread — all the nations descending from this one family, the human race renewed and multiplied from Shem, Ham, and Japheth. The verse displays the peopled earth, before Noah's vineyard (v.20).
20:
"And Noah began to be an husbandman, and he planted a vineyard:" The witnesses note Noah becoming a husbandman and planting a vineyard. On "Noah began to be an husbandman," the grammatical voices note the tillage. Gill and Clarke note that "Noah began to be an husbandman (a man of the soil)" — Noah took up the cultivation of the ground; he became a tiller of the soil, a farmer; after the flood, Noah devoted himself to husbandry, tilling the renewed earth; the honest, useful labor of agriculture.

On "he planted a vineyard," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the vineyard. Calvin and Gill note that Noah "planted a vineyard" — among his husbandry, he planted a vineyard and cultivated the vine; the planting of the vine was a lawful, useful work (wine being a gift of God, Psalm 104:15); the vineyard would yield the wine of which Noah drank (v.21); the cultivation of the vine after the flood.

On the vineyard, the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the occasion: the planting of the vineyard, in itself lawful and good, became the occasion of Noah's sin (v.21) — not that the vine or wine is evil, but that Noah, perhaps not yet knowing its strength (or through carelessness), fell into drunkenness; the gift of God abused through intemperance; the occasion of the sad incident that follows. The verse displays Noah becoming a husbandman and planting a vineyard.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the husbandry — "Noah began to be an husbandman," Noah taking up the tillage of the soil (Gill, Clarke); "he planted a vineyard," the lawful cultivation of the vine (Psalm 104:15, Calvin, Gill); the vineyard the occasion of Noah's sin (v.21, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the husbandman Noah. The verse marks the Noah: husbandman. The verse displays the vineyard: planted. The verse displays the labor: agriculture. The verse displays the occasion: of sin. The husbandry displays Noah tilling the soil and planting the vine. The verse displays the Noah: tilling. The verse displays the vine: planted. The verse displays the occasion: coming. Noah becoming a husbandman and planting a vineyard display his honest labor and the occasion of his fall: Noah became a tiller of the soil and planted a vineyard — the lawful cultivation of the vine, which would yield the wine of which he drank, becoming the occasion of his sin. The verse displays the planted vineyard, with Noah's drunkenness (v.21).
21:
"And he drank of the wine, and was drunken; and he was uncovered within his tent." The witnesses note Noah's drunkenness and his uncovering within his tent. On "he drank of the wine, and was drunken," the grammatical voices note the sin. Gill and Clarke note that "he drank of the wine, and was drunken" — Noah drank of the wine of his vineyard and became drunken; the sad fall of the righteous patriarch into intemperance; whether through ignorance of the wine's strength (perhaps a first experience of fermented wine) or through carelessness, Noah was overcome; the sin of drunkenness recorded faithfully, even of a holy man.

On "was drunken," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and reformed note the patriarch's fall. Clarke and Gill note that even Noah, just and perfect in his generations (6:9) and walking with God, fell into this sin; the Scripture does not hide the faults of its saints; Noah's drunkenness is a warning of the frailty of the best of men and the danger of intemperance; the example of even the godly falling, that none might presume.

On "he was uncovered within his tent," the grammatical-historical Ellicott and witnesses note the uncovering: "he was uncovered within his tent" — in his drunkenness Noah lay uncovered (his nakedness exposed) within his tent; the drunkenness led to his shameful, unseemly exposure; though within his tent (in private), his condition was seen by Ham (v.22); the indecency that drunkenness brings; the occasion of Ham's sin. The verse displays Noah's drunkenness and his uncovering within his tent.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the drunkenness — "he drank of the wine, and was drunken," Noah's sad fall into intemperance (Gill, Clarke); "was drunken," even the righteous patriarch falling, a warning of human frailty (6:9, Clarke, Gill); "he was uncovered within his tent," the shameful exposure that drunkenness brought (Ellicott). The verse displays the drunken Noah. The verse marks the wine: drunk. The verse displays the drunkenness: Noah's. The verse displays the uncovering: shameful. The verse displays the frailty: of the best. The fall displays the righteous Noah overcome by wine. The verse displays the Noah: drunken. The verse displays the exposure: uncovered. The verse displays the warning: of intemperance. Noah's drunkenness and his uncovering within his tent display the fall of the righteous and the shame of intemperance: Noah drank of the wine, was drunken, and lay uncovered in his tent — the sad fall of the holy patriarch, a warning of the frailty of the best of men and the indecency that drunkenness brings. The verse displays the uncovered Noah, seen by Ham (v.22).
22:
"And Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and told his two brethren without." The witnesses note Ham's sin in seeing and publishing his father's nakedness. On "Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father," the grammatical voices note the sin. Gill and Clarke note that "Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father" — Ham saw his father lying naked and drunk; the sin was not the mere seeing (which might have been accidental) but his irreverent, unfilial response — he gazed without shame, and (instead of covering his father) went to mock; the dishonoring of his father; Ham's lack of reverence and natural affection.

On "saw the nakedness of his father," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke notes the irreverence. Clarke and Gill note that Ham, seeing his father's shame, did not cover it or grieve, but looked on with irreverence and (it seems) delight; some traditions read more into the act, but the text marks his unfilial, mocking spirit; Ham failed in the honor due to a father; his sin was against the fifth commandment in spirit.

On "told his two brethren without," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the publishing: "told his two brethren without" — Ham went out and told his two brothers, publishing his father's shame; instead of covering it, he spread it; he made his father's disgrace a matter of talk and mockery; the aggravation of his sin — not only seeing but publishing and deriding; the contrast with Shem and Japheth, who covered their father (v.23). The verse displays Ham's sin in seeing and publishing his father's nakedness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sin — "Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father," Ham's irreverent gazing on his father's shame (Gill, Clarke); "saw the nakedness of his father," his unfilial, mocking spirit, dishonoring his father (Clarke, Gill); "told his two brethren without," his publishing and deriding his father's shame, the aggravation of his sin (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays Ham's sin. The verse marks the Ham: saw. The verse displays the nakedness: of his father. The verse displays the publishing: to his brethren. The verse displays the irreverence: unfilial. The sin displays Ham dishonoring and mocking his father. The verse displays the Ham: irreverent. The verse displays the shame: published. The verse displays the dishonor: of his father. Ham's sin in seeing and publishing his father's nakedness displays his unfilial irreverence: Ham saw his father's nakedness and told his brethren without — not covering but publishing and mocking his father's shame, dishonoring his father, in contrast to Shem and Japheth. The verse displays Ham's sin, against which his brothers act (v.23).
23:
"And Shem and Japheth took a garment, and laid it upon both their shoulders, and went backward, and covered the nakedness of their father; and their faces were backward, and they saw not their father's nakedness." The witnesses note Shem and Japheth covering their father's nakedness with reverence. On "Shem and Japheth took a garment, and laid it upon both their shoulders," the grammatical voices note the reverent care. Gill and JFB note that "Shem and Japheth took a garment, and laid it upon both their shoulders" — the two brothers took a garment to cover their father; they carried it on both their shoulders, ready to lay it over him; their loving, reverent care to hide their father's shame; the contrast with Ham, who published it.

On "went backward, and covered the nakedness of their father," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the delicacy. Calvin and Gill note that they "went backward, and covered the nakedness of their father" — they walked backward toward their father, so as not to see his shame, and covered him; their delicate, modest care to honor their father even in his disgrace; the reverence and filial piety of Shem and Japheth.

On "their faces were backward, and they saw not their father's nakedness," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the modesty: "their faces were backward, and they saw not their father's nakedness" — they turned their faces away, that they might not look on their father's shame; their studious avoidance of seeing what Ham had gazed upon; the honoring of their father, covering his fault in love (1 Peter 4:8, "charity shall cover the multitude of sins"); the pattern of filial reverence and of covering, rather than publishing, the faults of others. The verse displays Shem and Japheth covering their father's nakedness with reverence.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the care — "Shem and Japheth took a garment, and laid it upon both their shoulders," the brothers' reverent care to cover their father (Gill, JFB); "went backward, and covered the nakedness of their father," their delicate, modest covering (Calvin, Gill); "their faces were backward, and they saw not their father's nakedness," their studious avoidance of seeing his shame, honoring their father (1 Peter 4:8, Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the reverent brothers. The verse marks the garment: taken. The verse displays the covering: backward. The verse displays the faces: turned away. The verse displays the reverence: filial. The care displays Shem and Japheth honoring their father in his shame. The verse displays the brothers: reverent. The verse displays the nakedness: covered. The verse displays the honor: shown. Shem and Japheth covering their father's nakedness with reverence display their filial piety: they took a garment, went backward, and covered their father, their faces turned away — their loving, modest care to honor their father and hide his shame, in contrast to Ham; the pattern of covering, not publishing, a fault. The verse displays the honored father, who blesses them (vv.26-27).
24:
"And Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his younger son had done unto him." The witnesses note Noah awaking and knowing what had been done to him. On "Noah awoke from his wine," the grammatical voices note the awaking. Ellicott and Gill note that "Noah awoke from his wine" — Noah came to himself, sobered from his drunkenness; he awoke and recovered his senses; the patriarch, recovered, learned what had passed during his stupor; the awaking that brought the knowledge of Ham's sin and his brothers' kindness.

On "knew what his younger son had done unto him," interpreters note the knowledge and the identity. The puritan Henry and reformed Calvin note that Noah "knew what his younger son had done unto him" — Noah came to know (whether by report or by the Spirit of prophecy) what Ham had done; "his younger son" is generally taken to mean Ham (though the term has prompted discussion of birth order); Noah learned of Ham's irreverence and his brothers' reverent covering.

On "what his younger son had done," the grammatical-historical note the prophetic discernment: Noah, awaking, knew Ham's sin, and (moved by the Spirit) pronounced the prophetic curse and blessing (vv.25-27); the knowledge of what was done led to the prophecy; Noah, as a patriarch and prophet, spoke not in mere personal resentment but by divine inspiration, foretelling the destinies of his sons' descendants; the awaking and knowledge are the occasion of the prophecy. The verse displays Noah awaking and knowing what had been done to him.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the awaking — "Noah awoke from his wine," Noah sobered and recovered from his drunkenness (Ellicott, Gill); "knew what his younger son had done unto him," Noah coming to know Ham's sin (his younger son, generally Ham, Henry, Calvin); the knowledge leading to the prophetic curse and blessing, spoken by inspiration (vv.25-27, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the awakened Noah. The verse marks the awaking: from his wine. The verse displays the knowledge: of Ham's sin. The verse displays the son: younger (Ham). The verse displays the prophecy: to come. The awaking displays Noah recovered and knowing what was done. The verse displays the Noah: awake. The verse displays the sin: known. The verse displays the prophecy: prompted. Noah awaking and knowing what had been done to him display the occasion of the prophecy: Noah awoke from his wine and knew what his younger son had done — recovered from his drunkenness and learning of Ham's sin and his brothers' kindness, which moved him to pronounce, by inspiration, the prophetic curse and blessing. The verse displays the awakened Noah, who curses Canaan (v.25).
25:
"And he said, Cursed be Canaan; a servant of servants shall he be unto his brethren." The witnesses note Noah's curse on Canaan — the crux of why Canaan, and not Ham. On "Cursed be Canaan," interpreters ask why Canaan is cursed rather than Ham (the offender). The grammatical-historical Ellicott and reformed Calvin note the difficulty and the readings: Ham sinned (v.22), yet the curse falls on Canaan, Ham's son. Several explanations are offered: (1) the curse on Ham falls through his son Canaan — Ham, who dishonored his father, is punished in his posterity, in the son perhaps dearest to him; (2) Canaan himself shared in or prompted the sin (some traditions hold Canaan was involved); (3) the curse is prophetic of the Canaanites — Noah, by the Spirit, foretells the degradation and subjection of Canaan's descendants (the Canaanites), who would be dispossessed and made servants by Israel (Shem's line) and others; Ham is passed over, and the prophecy fixes on Canaan, whose posterity's later wickedness and subjection are foretold. (Clarke notes the Arabic version reads "Ham the father of Canaan," but this is rejected.)

On "a servant of servants shall he be unto his brethren," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and witnesses note the servitude. Clarke and Gill note that "a servant of servants (the lowest, most abject servant) shall he be unto his brethren" — Canaan's descendants would be the most abject of servants, subject to the descendants of Shem and Japheth; the prophecy was fulfilled in the subjugation of the Canaanites by Israel (Joshua) and their later servitude; "of servants" (a Hebrew superlative) marks the depth of the subjection; the prophetic foretelling of the Canaanites' degraded condition.

On the curse, the grammatical-historical note that it is prophecy, not mere imprecation: Noah, by inspiration, foretells (does not arbitrarily decree) the destiny of Canaan's line; the curse answers to the later wickedness of the Canaanites; (the verse concerns the Canaanites, not a racial curse on any people). The verse displays Noah's curse on Canaan.

The witnesses agree the substance: Noah, by inspiration, pronounced Canaan a servant of servants to his brethren, fulfilled in the subjection of the Canaanites (Ellicott, Calvin, Clarke, Gill). The curse bears a crux: why Canaan, not Ham? — the curse on Ham falling through his son Canaan (Ham punished in his posterity), or Canaan sharing in the sin, or (chiefly) the prophecy fixing on the Canaanites, whose later degradation and subjection to Shem's line it foretells. The verse displays the Canaan crux. The verse marks the curse: on Canaan. The verse displays the debate: why Canaan, not Ham. The verse displays the servitude: of servants. The verse displays the prophecy: of the Canaanites. The curse displays Noah foretelling the subjection of Canaan's line. The verse displays the Canaan: cursed. The verse displays the servant: of servants. The verse displays the prophecy: fulfilled. Noah's curse on Canaan displays the prophetic doom and a crux: cursed be Canaan, a servant of servants to his brethren — read as Ham's curse falling through his son, or Canaan's own share in the sin, or the prophecy of the Canaanites' degradation and subjection to Shem's line; the servitude fulfilled in the subjugation of the Canaanites. The verse displays the cursed Canaan, with the blessing of Shem (v.26).
26:
"And he said, Blessed be the LORD God of Shem; and Canaan shall be his servant." The witnesses note Noah blessing the LORD God of Shem. On "Blessed be the LORD God of Shem," the grammatical voices note the blessing. Ellicott and Gill note that "Blessed be the LORD God of Shem" — Noah blesses not Shem directly, but the LORD (Jehovah) as the God of Shem; the blessing of Shem is expressed as a blessing of God, who would be in a special covenant relation to Shem's line; the highest blessing — that the LORD would be Shem's God; the line of promise (and the Messiah) would come through Shem.

On "the LORD God of Shem," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the covenant blessing. Calvin and Gill note that the LORD being "the God of Shem" marks Shem's posterity as the chosen line — the people of God (Israel) and the line of the promised seed would descend from Shem; the covenant relation, the true religion, and the Messiah belong to Shem's line; the spiritual blessing surpassing the temporal; God himself is Shem's portion.

On "and Canaan shall be his servant," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the servitude: "and Canaan shall be his servant" — Canaan (the Canaanites) would be servant to Shem (Shem's descendants, Israel); the prophecy of the subjection of Canaan to Shem's line, fulfilled when Israel conquered and subjected the Canaanites; the curse on Canaan (v.25) repeated in relation to Shem's blessing; the line of promise exalted, the cursed line subjected. The verse displays Noah blessing the LORD God of Shem.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the blessing — "Blessed be the LORD God of Shem," Noah blessing the LORD as Shem's God, the highest blessing (Ellicott, Gill); "the LORD God of Shem," Shem's line as the chosen, the line of the promise and the Messiah (Calvin, Gill); "and Canaan shall be his servant," Canaan subject to Shem's line, fulfilled in Israel (Wesley, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the blessed Shem. The verse marks the blessing: the LORD God of Shem. The verse displays the line: of promise. The verse displays the Canaan: servant. The verse displays the Messiah: of Shem. The blessing displays the LORD as Shem's God, the line of promise. The verse displays the Shem: blessed. The verse displays the LORD: his God. The verse displays the Canaan: subjected. Noah blessing the LORD God of Shem displays the covenant blessing of Shem's line: blessed be the LORD God of Shem, and Canaan shall be his servant — the highest blessing, that the LORD would be Shem's God, the line of promise and the Messiah coming through Shem, with Canaan subjected to his line. The verse displays the blessed Shem, with the enlarging of Japheth (v.27).
27:
"God shall enlarge Japheth, and he shall dwell in the tents of Shem; and Canaan shall be his servant." The witnesses note the blessing of Japheth — the crux of who dwells in the tents of Shem. On "God shall enlarge Japheth," the grammatical voices note the enlargement. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott note that "God shall enlarge Japheth" — there is a wordplay (Japheth/yapht, "enlarge"); God would enlarge Japheth, giving his descendants a wide extent, a large possession; the posterity of Japheth (the peoples of Europe and the north/west) would be numerous and widely spread; the enlargement of Japheth fulfilled in the great spread of his descendants over the earth.

On "he shall dwell in the tents of Shem," interpreters divide over the subject. The reformed Gill notes the disputed question: who dwells in the tents of Shem? The dominant Christian reading takes Japheth as the subject — Japheth (the Gentiles, his descendants) would dwell in the tents of Shem; that is, the Gentiles would be brought into the spiritual blessings of Shem's line — into the church, sharing in the covenant and the Messiah who came of Shem (the calling of the Gentiles, Ephesians 2-3; Romans 11); Japheth dwelling in Shem's tents is the Gentiles partaking of the gospel and the blessing of Abraham. A second reading takes God as the subject — "God shall dwell in the tents of Shem" — God would dwell among Shem's posterity (in the tabernacle and temple, and in Christ of Shem's line); the Shekinah and the incarnation in Shem's line.

On "and Canaan shall be his servant," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the servitude: "and Canaan shall be his servant" — Canaan would be servant to Japheth also (as to Shem, v.26); the Canaanites subjected to both lines; the curse on Canaan repeated; the subjection of Canaan to the blessed lines of Shem and Japheth. The verse displays the blessing of Japheth.

The witnesses agree the substance: God would enlarge Japheth, and (the dominant reading) Japheth would dwell in the tents of Shem, with Canaan as servant (Keil, Ellicott, Gill, Wesley). The phrase "he shall dwell in the tents of Shem" bears a crux: Japheth is the subject — the Gentiles brought into the spiritual blessings of Shem's line, the calling of the Gentiles into the church (the dominant reading) vs God is the subject — God dwelling in the tents of Shem (the Shekinah and the incarnation in Shem's line). The verse displays the tents-of-Shem crux. The verse marks the Japheth: enlarged. The verse displays the debate: Japheth or God dwelling. The verse displays the tents: of Shem. The verse displays the Canaan: servant. The blessing displays Japheth enlarged and brought into Shem's blessing. The verse displays the Japheth: blessed. The verse displays the Gentiles: ingrafted. The verse displays the Shem: shared. The blessing of Japheth displays the enlargement and the ingathering, and a crux: God shall enlarge Japheth, and he shall dwell in the tents of Shem — read by the dominant view as Japheth (the Gentiles) brought into the spiritual blessings of Shem's line (the calling of the Gentiles, Ephesians 2-3), and by another as God dwelling in Shem's tents (the Shekinah and the incarnation); with Canaan servant to both. The verse displays the enlarged Japheth, closing Noah's prophecy (v.28).
28:
"And Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years." The very brief verse notes Noah's long life after the flood. The grammatical voices note the years. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years" — Noah lived three hundred and fifty years after the flood; the long life of the patriarch continued into the renewed world; Noah saw many generations of his descendants multiply and the earth repeopled; the bridge between the old world and the new lived on for centuries.

On "after the flood three hundred and fifty years," the puritan Henry notes the continuance. Henry and Gill note that Noah's long life after the flood (three hundred and fifty years) allowed him to see the growth of the nations from his sons; he lived to witness the repopulation of the earth and (by the chronology) nearly to the time of Abraham; the long-lived patriarch's continuance in the renewed world.

On the years, the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the witness: Noah's three hundred and fifty years after the flood made him a living witness, through many generations, of the flood and God's covenant; he could testify to his descendants of the judgment and the deliverance; the patriarch's long life preserved the memory and the knowledge of God in the new world. The verse displays Noah's long life after the flood.

The very brief comment matches the very brief verse. The witnesses agree: the years — "Noah lived after the flood three hundred and fifty years," Noah's long life continuing into the renewed world (Keil, Gill); the long life allowing him to see the nations grow from his sons (Henry, Gill); the patriarch a living witness of the flood and covenant (Calvin, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the long-lived Noah. The verse marks the years: 350 after the flood. The verse displays the life: continued. The verse displays the witness: of the flood. The verse displays the world: renewed. The long life displays Noah continuing in the renewed world. The verse displays the Noah: living. The verse displays the years: many. The verse displays the witness: borne. Noah's long life after the flood displays the patriarch's continuance in the renewed world: Noah lived three hundred and fifty years after the flood — the long-lived patriarch seeing the earth repeopled from his sons, a living witness of the flood and God's covenant through many generations. The verse displays the long-lived Noah, before his death (v.29).
29:
"And all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years: and he died." The witnesses note the total of Noah's years and his death. On "all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years," the grammatical voices note the sum. Ellicott and Gill note that "all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years" — the full span of Noah's life, nine hundred and fifty years (six hundred before the flood, three hundred and fifty after); among the longest-lived of the patriarchs; the great age of Noah, who bridged the old and new worlds.

On "nine hundred and fifty years," the reformed JFB and witnesses note the long life. JFB and Gill note that Noah's nine hundred and fifty years is among the longest in Scripture (after Methuselah and Jared); the great age of the patriarch, spanning the flood and the early generations of the renewed world; the long life now drawing, like all, to its end.

On "and he died," the wesleyan-arminian Clarke and grammatical-historical note the refrain: "and he died" — like the patriarchs before the flood (ch. 5), Noah, the righteous and long-lived, died at last; the sentence of death (3:19) takes effect even on Noah; the refrain that tolled through the genealogy of ch. 5 sounds once more over Noah; the righteous patriarch, who walked with God and was saved through the flood, ended his long life in the grave, in hope of the resurrection. The verse displays the total of Noah's years and his death.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the sum — "all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years," the full span of Noah's great life (Ellicott, Gill); "nine hundred and fifty years," among the longest lives in Scripture (JFB, Gill); "and he died," the refrain of mortality, even Noah dying (3:19; ch. 5, Clarke, grammatical-historical). The verse displays the dead Noah. The verse marks the years: 950. The verse displays the life: great. The verse displays the death: and he died. The verse displays the refrain: of mortality. The total displays Noah's long life ending in death. The verse displays the days: numbered. The verse displays the Noah: died. The verse displays the sentence: fulfilled. The total of Noah's years and his death display the close of the patriarch's life: all the days of Noah were nine hundred and fifty years, and he died — the great life of the righteous patriarch, who bridged the old and new worlds, ending at last in the grave, the refrain of mortality sounding once more. The verse closes the history of Noah, before the generations of his sons (ch.10).
